issues.name: Consolidate endpoint discovery: one walker shared by build-time + runtime
issues.description:
## Problem

Today the worker discovers `@inference`-decorated classes TWICE, via two separately maintained code paths that walk the same `inspect.getmembers(module)` loop looking for the same `__gen_worker_endpoint_spec__` attribute:

1. **Build-time** (`gen_worker/discovery/discover.py::discover_functions`) — runs once in the Dockerfile (`RUN python -m gen_worker.discovery > /app/.tensorhub/endpoint.lock`), writes a JSON manifest of every function it found (names, schemas, resources, bindings). Tensorhub reads this manifest at build success to populate the catalog row.

2. **Runtime** (`gen_worker/worker.py::Worker._discover_handlers`) — runs every worker boot, instantiates each `@inference` class, registers callable handler methods in the dispatch tables (`_request_specs`, `_training_specs`, `_conversion_class_specs`).

The two passes are not connected. If they disagree, the worker registers with the orchestrator but rejects every dispatched request with `Unknown function requested`, and there is no in-built check to surface the divergence (a stop-gap manifest-vs-runtime ERROR log was added in 1e70e0f-ish but it's defense-in-depth, not a structural fix).

This duplication caused the 2026-05-16 conversion endpoint outage: the build-time walk found 13 functions in the `conversion` package (classes defined in submodules, re-exported via `__init__.py`'s `from .main import *`). The runtime walk's strict `cls.__module__ == module.__name__` check rejected every one as a 'third-party re-export' and the worker booted with zero handlers. Workaround patched the runtime walk to accept submodule re-exports; the underlying duplication remains.

## Design

Extract ONE walker into a new module (e.g. `gen_worker/discovery/walk.py`):

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class FoundEndpointClass:
    cls: type                          # live Python class
    declared_module: str               # cls.__module__
    qualname: str                      # cls.__qualname__
    endpoint_spec: object              # __gen_worker_endpoint_spec__
    function_methods: list[FoundFunctionMethod]

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class FoundFunctionMethod:
    attr: str                          # attribute name on the class
    method: Callable                   # bound-method descriptor
    function_spec: object              # __gen_worker_function_spec__ from @inference.function

def find_endpoint_classes(module_names: list[str]) -> list[FoundEndpointClass]:
    '''The single source of truth for endpoint discovery. Walks each
    user module (and its submodules, when present), returns every
    @inference / @training / @conversion class with its decorated
    function methods attached. Handles re-exports, submodule walks,
    and dedup by class identity.'''
    ...
```

Then:

- `discover_functions()` (build-time) calls `find_endpoint_classes(...)`, serializes each   entry's metadata (names, schemas, resources, model bindings) to JSON for the lockfile.
- `Worker._discover_handlers()` (runtime) calls `find_endpoint_classes(...)`, instantiates   each class, registers each function method in the dispatch tables.

Both consumers get the SAME list of classes from the SAME walk. By construction they can't diverge.

## What to delete after the refactor

- `Worker._discover_classes_in_module` (added in 1e70e0f / its successor commit) —   the submodule-re-export prefix check moves into the unified walker.
- `Worker._discover_classes_via_submodule_walk` (same patch) — the fallback `pkgutil.walk_packages`   scan likewise moves into the walker (or becomes always-on, not just a fallback).
- `Worker._verify_manifest_function_coverage` (same patch) — divergence becomes impossible,   so the ERROR-log defense becomes dead code.
- The duplicated `inspect.getmembers(module)` loop in `discover.py`. The build-time code   shrinks to 'call the walker, serialize each result'.

Net: less code (the refactor removes more than it adds), one fewer bug class.

## Acceptance

- `gen_worker/discovery/walk.py` (new) exports `find_endpoint_classes(module_names)` returning   `list[FoundEndpointClass]`.
- `gen_worker/discovery/discover.py::discover_functions` calls the walker; the manifest   it writes has identical schema to today's lockfile (no consumer changes in tensorhub).
- `Worker._discover_handlers` calls the walker; in-process behavior identical to today.
- The submodule-walk, prefix-check, and manifest-verification paths added on 2026-05-16   are deleted (their behaviors are now intrinsic to the unified walker).
- Existing tests in `tests/test_discovery_*.py` (including the regression tests added on   2026-05-16 for submodule re-exports) continue to pass against the unified walker.
- The conversion endpoint (training-endpoints/conversion) discovers all 13 functions at   both build-time and runtime against the unified walker. Live-stack smoke: rebuild   the conversion image, redeploy, fire an `hf-clone` request — completes successfully.

## Why this isn't urgent

Today's band-aid patch (1e70e0f) makes runtime discovery match build-time for the specific layout that broke conversion. The structural duplication is still there but doesn't actively bite — until someone hits another layout edge case. The refactor removes the failure mode entirely by design, but it's a structural cleanup, not a bugfix.

## Related

- 1e70e0f and successor commits — runtime discovery band-aids.
- conversion endpoint at training-endpoints/conversion — the layout that triggered   the discovery divergence in production.

issues.name: Adaptive sizing for MAX_CONCURRENT_UPLOADS (drop the hardcoded constant)
issues.description:
Today `MAX_CONCURRENT_UPLOADS = 4` in `_concurrent_upload.py` is a hardcoded empirical default — chosen because 4 concurrent files saturated a 10 GbE / NVMe / 8-core test host without thrashing the BLAKE3 worker threads. On slower disks or hosts with more cores the right number is different. User feedback (#269 closeout, 2026-05-16): hardcoding any one number is wrong; the pool should saturate the available bandwidth/CPU without the operator having to tune anything.

## Design (sketch)

Replace the constant with an adaptive sizer:

1. Start at N=1 (or 2). Each file completes; record elapsed time + bytes uploaded.
2. Compute aggregate throughput over a sliding window (last 4-8 files).
3. After each file finishes, decide:
   - If recent throughput is still trending up vs the previous window, increase N by 1 (cap at some sanity ceiling like 16).
   - If throughput plateaued or fell, hold N steady.
   - If error rate ticked up (5xx, timeouts), decrease N by 1.
4. Combined with the existing `BudgetGate` (which back-pressures on capability budget), the sizer never
   over-commits in-flight bytes either.

## Constraints

- **Not env-configurable.** User explicitly does not want a knob.
- **No global state**. Each clone job's pool sizes itself independently from a clean baseline (different   workloads have different sweet spots).
- **Bounded**: sanity ceiling so a runaway sizer can't try 100 concurrent uploads on a degenerate workload.

## Why this isn't urgent

The hardcoded N=4 is good enough today: clones complete in reasonable wall-time on the live e2e stack, the back-pressure gate prevents catastrophic over-commit, and most clone workloads are 2-10 file batches where N=4 saturates anyway. The adaptive sizer is a refinement for the long tail where hosts vary significantly.

## Related

- tensorhub #269 (closed) — original upload throughput work, capability-budget back-pressure, blake3 swap.
- The `BudgetGate` design in `_concurrent_upload.py` is the right back-pressure primitive for the adaptive
  sizer to compose with: byte budgets enforce a hard ceiling, concurrency tuning runs within that ceiling.
